text: The video shows a large incarcerated indirect inguinal hernia in a sixty-three-year-old patient. The surgeon reduces a portion of the small bowel upon entering the abdomen.
The surgeon works from medial to lateral towards Bogros' space, using touch cautery to carefully push nerves laterally while maintaining a thin flap and pulling it down.

The surgeon transitions from the flap to the supravesicular space, extending the dissection medially beyond the symphysis pubis. The infrared epigastric vessels are visible, with the indirect inguinal hernia located laterally to these vessels.

The surgeon sweeps the fat laterally, ensuring it covers the nerves, while using touch cautery to separate tissue. The Maryland dissector is used to apply traction and counter traction, pulling down and to the left, then down and to the right.

The surgeon uses cautery to dissect down into the hernia sac, pulling up and to the left while sweeping vessels and fat laterally. The indirect hernia sac is tugged and pulled down at an oblique angle, revealing a cord lipoma that is released and retracts back inside.

The surgeon sweeps tissue downwards, carefully avoiding the vas deferens, which is visible at the ten o'clock position. The surgeon then works back up laterally, maintaining a clear view of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon creates a lateral flap by sweeping the lateral fascia to cover the nerves, preparing the area for mesh placement. Using touch cautery, the surgeon sweeps tissue down, revealing a large hernia extending to the deep internal ring and crossing medially. The surgeon carefully navigates around the vas and deferential vessels to visualize the iliac vein.

The surgeon continues the dissection, focusing on the medial portion near the deep internal ring, which is critical to prevent recurrence. The area gradually comes into view as the surgeon carefully maneuvers the tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the corner of the surgical field, teasing the tissue flap down while ensuring ample space for mesh placement. The iliac vein is visible in the background as the surgeon continues to secure the flap, aiming to prevent mesh buckling.

The surgeon places two sutures for high lateral fixation of a very large indirect hernia, positioning them just medial to the coronamortis distal vein to ensure stable mesh placement and reduce the risk of migration.
The surgeon visually identifies the muscle and proceeds to close the area using a standard one-hand technique, ensuring precise placement without blind suturing.

I like to, at the end, I mean, I always try to, if it's a large indirect or even some of the large direct hernias, I'll always feel compelled to suture the hernia sac back onto the flap. A couple times in my career, I think this is, you know, probably inguinal hernia number one thousand or so. I've had to go back where I just kind of left that flap kind of flopping in the breeze. And then when it happened, I had to go back. It caused a bowel obstruction. One caused an internal hernia and I was cutting them off. But as you know, they're very vascular down towards the base. So I stopped cutting them off a couple hundred hernias ago.

The surgeon continues suturing the flap, ensuring there are no loops that could lead to entanglement with intestinal tissue. The sutures are applied in a purse-string manner to close any defects and prevent recurrence of the flap.
